retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Increase uptake of anti-inflammatory foods: It is important to avoid eating simple sugars, refined carbohydrates, high-glycemic foods, trans fats, and hydrogenated oils. Consuming whole grains, natural foods, plenty of vegetables and fruits such as avocados, cherries, kale, and fatty fish like salmon is helpful in defeating inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Noncompetitive Inhibitor -- Clinical Significance -- Chronic Disease
retrieved_docs.content: Type 2 diabetes mellitus (T2DM) is 1 of the most common chronic diseases plaguing the healthcare system with high morbidity and mortality. While vast sums of time and money have gone towards developing safe, effective medical therapies for T2DM, these medications are not without side effects and are tolerated differently on an individual basis. Research continues to investigate “less invasive” means of controlling blood sugar. Inhibition of intestinal enzymes responsible for the breakdown of dietary sugar (such as alpha-glucosidase and alpha-amylase) effectively prevents postprandial hyperglycemia in the diabetic population. Rosha grass ( Cymbopogon martinii ) has shown activity as a non-competitive alpha-glucosidase inhibitor, explaining its effectiveness as a traditional Indian treatment for T2DM. [12] Similarly, a plant used as folk treatment throughout Indonesia for T2DM was found to possess a component that acts as a noncompetitive alpha-amylase inhibitor, effectively lowering blood glucose levels in diabetic rats. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Inflammation -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Low-glycemic diet:  Diet with a high glycemic index is related to high risk of stroke, coronary heart disease, and type 2 diabetes mellitus. It is beneficial to limit the consumption of inflammation-promoting foods like sodas, refined carbohydrates, fructose corn syrup in a diet.

retrieved_docs.title: Systemic Inflammatory Response Syndrome -- Treatment / Management -- Blood Glucose Control
retrieved_docs.content: Maintaining blood glucose levels below 180 mg/dL remains the recommended target based on the findings of the NICE-SUGAR trial, which demonstrated improved outcomes with moderate glucose control. Tight glycemic control in the range of 80 to 110 mg/dL is no longer advised due to the significantly increased risk of hypoglycemia and associated adverse events.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity and Comorbid Conditions -- Nursing, Allied Health, and Interprofessional Team Monitoring -- Naturopathy in Obesity Management
retrieved_docs.content: Forskolin: Forskolin is an herb from the mint family and is believed to increase the production of cyclic adenosine monophosphate, which may aid in fat breakdown and promote weight loss. Fiber supplements: Fiber supplements that help in weight management are listed below. Psyllium husk: Psyllium is a soluble fiber that can promote feelings of fullness, regulate bowel movements, and support healthy digestion, which may aid in weight management. Glucomannan: Glucomannan is a soluble fiber derived from the konjac root. This absorbs water in the digestive tract, creating a feeling of fullness and reducing calorie intake. Probiotics: Probiotics are increasingly studied for their potential role in weight management. Certain strains of probiotics may help regulate gut microbiota composition, which could influence metabolic processes and inflammation related to obesity. Lactobacillus gasseri and L rhamnosus are among the strains studied for their potential benefits in weight management.

retrieved_docs.title: Obesity Supplements -- Function -- Psyllium
retrieved_docs.content: Psyllium seed is a soluble fiber supplement derived from the husk of Plantago psyllium . Psyllium is purported to promote weight loss by acting as a bulking agent in the gut, which results in delayed gastric emptying and increased satiety. Fiber-enriched meals decrease the hunger hormone ghrelin and increase the satiety hormone peptide YY. [69] This finding suggests that fiber-rich meals might reduce appetite and increase satiety, although the data are inconsistent. Many human studies show no improvement in body weight and composition after psyllium consumption. One early study of psyllium supplementation showed improved glucose and lipid control compared to placebo in 125 overweight patients with type 2 diabetes, although no weight loss occurred. [70] Recent studies have demonstrated similar results with improved metabolic parameters without statistically significant weight reduction. A study in overweight and obese individuals noted a dose-response relationship with psyllium supplementation, with a dose greater than 30 g of fiber per day leading to the most robust weight loss effect compared to a placebo. [71] A more recent trial in patients with type 2 diabetes supported these positive findings, demonstrating that 10.5 g of psyllium supplementation daily for 8 weeks significantly reduced body weight, waist circumference, and hip circumference. [72] Psyllium is less readily fermented and does not cause as much flatulence and abdominal distension as other fiber supplements. Still, the most commonly reported side effects are gastrointestinal, including flatulence, abdominal pain, diarrhea, constipation, and nausea. Generally, psyllium is well tolerated for short-term use without serious adverse effects. [73]

retrieved_docs.title: Anti-Inflammatory Diets -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Many anti-inflammatory diets also emphasize the consumption of whole grains (1 to 2 servings per meal), nuts (1 to 2 servings per day), and legumes (1 to 2 servings per week). [16] These foods promote a reduced glycemic index burden. A reduced glycemic index burden has been positively associated with a reduction in inflammation, leading to a reduction in the development of malignancies. [8] [1] The increased consumption of whole grains and the reduced intake of refined sugars result in a lower total delivered glucose load and reduced insulin production. Insulin, insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1), estrogens, and androgens are powerful cellular mitogens linked with the development and growth of several tumors. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Anti-Inflammatory Diets -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Consuming the high amounts of refined grain and sugar products typical of a proinflammatory diet increases the total delivered glucose load, which leads to a compensatory increase in insulin production. [8] Insulin is a proinflammatory hormone; chronic hyperinsulinemia increases the risk of developing metabolic syndrome. [8] Proinflammatory diets are high in sodium and low in potassium due to the increased intake of table salt and processed foods containing added salt and reduced daily potassium-rich foods like fruits and vegetables. [8] This inversion of potassium and sodium concentrations contributes to the development and progression of diseases such as hypertension, stroke, kidney stones, and gastrointestinal cancers. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2075
retrieved_docs.content: Glibenklamid and glimepiride are both members of the sulfonylurea class of oral agents used for blood glucose lowering. As sulfonylureas, they act by stimulating pancreatic insulin secretion, thereby helping to control hyperglycemia in patients with diabetes mellitus. Recognizing that glibenklamid and glimepiride belong to the same therapeutic group informs clinicians about their similar mechanisms of action, potential side effect profiles, and considerations for interchangeable use in diabetes management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2479

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2063
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues constitute a class of therapeutic agents that mimic the incretin hormone GLP‑1, enhancing glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, inhibiting glucagon release, slowing gastric emptying, and promoting satiety. Liraglutide is a member of this GLP‑1 analogue class, and its pharmacologic actions make it valuable for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus and for weight management in patients with obesity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1860
retrieved_docs.content: Plasma hyperosmolarity (ارتفاع أسمولالية البلازما) is clinically associated with elevated blood glucose (ارتفاع السكر في الدم) and is a common manifestation in both type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2). It is also linked to insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين) and can coexist with low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم). Dehydration (التجفاف) is mapped to plasma hyperosmolarity, indicating that fluid loss often precipitates the hyperosmolar state, and dehydration itself is clinically associated with acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد). Acute kidney failure is further clinically associated with type 1 diabetes, type 2 diabetes, insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes, acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ), dehydration, abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية), and low blood volume. These interconnections highlight that disturbances in fluid balance and severe hyperglycemia can lead to plasma hyperosmolarity, which together with diabetes‑related metabolic stress may precipitate acute kidney failure, often accompanied by hypovolemia, mental status changes, and pancreatic inflammation. Recognizing these linked clinical features is essential for timely diagnosis and comprehensive management of patients presenting with hyperosmolar, diabetic, or renal complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2818
retrieved_docs.content: فرط كوليسترول الدم (hypercholesterolemia) is clinically associated with مرض السكري نوع 2 (type 2 diabetes mellitus), indicating that elevated blood cholesterol frequently co‑occurs with this form of diabetes. In addition, الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ (acute pancreatitis) is clinically associated with فرط كوليسترول الدم (hypercholesterolemia), suggesting that high cholesterol levels may also be linked to the development of severe pancreatic inflammation. Together, these connections imply that hypercholesterolemia can serve as a common clinical link between type 2 diabetes and acute pancreatitis, underscoring the importance of lipid monitoring and management in diabetic patients to potentially mitigate the risk of pancreatitis and its complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 2 -- Community 1048
retrieved_docs.content: Insulin aspart, a human rapid‑acting insulin analog, exerts a physiologic effect of decreasing lipolysis and achieves this action by targeting the human insulin‑receptor (INSR) protein. Because of its ability to replace or supplement endogenous insulin, insulin aspart is used in the therapeutic management of both type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1) and type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 2).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 3 -- Community 1478
retrieved_docs.content: Glibenklamid is identified as a member of the class of Sulfonylureas for blood glucose lowering, indicating that it belongs to this group of oral antidiabetic agents that reduce plasma glucose levels by stimulating insulin secretion from the pancreas. As a sulfonylurea, glibenklamid plays a role in therapeutic strategies for managing hyperglycemia in patients with type 2 diabetes, underscoring its importance in achieving effective glycemic control.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 4 -- Community 2860
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with several important medical conditions. It is linked to type 1 diabetes (مرض السكري نوع 1), indicating that patients with this form of diabetes may be at increased risk for developing acute kidney failure and that renal impairment can complicate diabetes management. Acute kidney failure is also associated with acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ), reflecting a bidirectional relationship in which severe pancreatic inflammation can precipitate renal injury and renal dysfunction can exacerbate pancreatic disease. In addition, an abnormal psychological state (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية) is clinically associated with acute kidney failure, suggesting that mental health disturbances may coexist with, contribute to, or result from severe renal impairment. Finally, acute kidney failure is associated with insulin‑requiring type 2 diabetes (مرض السكري من النوع الثاني (2) المتطلب للأنسولين), underscoring the heightened vulnerability of patients with advanced type 2 diabetes to renal complications. Recognizing these interconnections is essential for comprehensive patient assessment, early detection of comorbidities, and coordinated management of both renal and metabolic or psychological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2841
retrieved_docs.content: Unstable angina (ذبحة غير مستقرة) and acute pancreatitis (الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياسِ الحادّ) are both clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Hypercholesterolemia, defined by elevated blood cholesterol levels, is a recognized risk factor for atherosclerotic disease that can precipitate unstable angina, and it may also contribute to inflammatory conditions such as acute pancreatitis. Recognizing these connections highlights the importance of effective lipid management to reduce the risk of both cardiovascular events and pancreatic inflammation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1919
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism include the SMQ titled “ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد” (new‑onset diabetes mellitus with elevated blood glucose), and this SMQ in turn comprises the clinical effect “تأثير إدرار البول” (increased urine output). This relationship indicates that new‑onset diabetes, classified within lipid metabolism disorders, is associated with the manifestation of polyuria, highlighting the interconnected nature of metabolic dysregulation and its renal‑related symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2629
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammatory small bowel disease is characterized by a chronic clinical course, indicating a prolonged and persistent disease trajectory. This chronic pattern of disease progression is similarly observed in secondary diabetes mellitus that is complicated by diabetic chronic kidney disease, where the condition also follows a chronic clinical course. The shared chronic nature of these distinct disorders underscores the importance of recognizing that patients with chronic inflammatory small bowel disease may be susceptible to metabolic disturbances such as secondary diabetes mellitus and its renal complications, necessitating vigilant monitoring and integrated management of both gastrointestinal and endocrine‑renal health.
